heading: (intro)
raw: Directed by Billy Wilder. Written by Larry Marcus, Billy Wilder, and Harry Kurnitz, based on a short story by Agatha Christie.
clean: Directed by Billy Wilder. Written by Larry Marcus, Billy Wilder, and Harry Kurnitz, based on a short story by Agatha Christie.

heading: Sir Wilfrid Robarts
raw: I am constantly surprised that women's hats do not provoke more murders.
clean: I am constantly surprised that women's hats do not provoke more murders.

heading: Miss Plimsoll
raw: [last lines; hands Sir Wilfrid his thermos bottle] Sir Wilfrid, you've forgotten your brandy!
clean: Sir Wilfrid, you've forgotten your brandy!

heading: Dialogue
raw: Leonard Vole: What are you looking for?
clean: What are you looking for?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Christine Vole: My accordion.
clean: My accordion.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Leonard Vole: [stepping on it] I think I've found it.
clean: I think I've found it.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Christine Vole: Step on it again. It's still breathing.
clean: Step on it again. It's still breathing.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Miss Plimsoll: Shall we roll up the window, Sir Wilfrid?
clean: Shall we roll up the window, Sir Wilfrid?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: Just roll up your mouth, you talk too much. If I'd known how much you talk I'd never have come out of my coma.
clean: Just roll up your mouth, you talk too much. If I'd known how much you talk I'd never have come out of my coma.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Miss Plimsoll: I almost married a lawyer once. I was in attendance when he had his appendectomy, and we became engaged as soon as he could sit up... and then peritonitis set in and he went just like that!
clean: I almost married a lawyer once. I was in attendance when he had his appendectomy, and we became engaged as soon as he could sit up... and then peritonitis set in and he went just like that!

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: He certainly was a lucky lawyer.
clean: He certainly was a lucky lawyer.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: Give me a match.
clean: Give me a match.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Leonard Vole: Sorry, I never carried them.
clean: Sorry, I never carried them.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: [to Mayhew] What? You said I'd like him.
clean: What? You said I'd like him.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Leonard Vole: But I do have a lighter.
clean: But I do have a lighter.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfird: You're quite right, Mayhew. I do like him.
clean: You're quite right, Mayhew. I do like him.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Leonard Vole: But this is England, where I thought you never arrest, let alone convict, people for crimes they have not committed.
clean: But this is England, where I thought you never arrest, let alone convict, people for crimes they have not committed.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: We try not to make a habit of it.
clean: We try not to make a habit of it.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: Be prepared for hysterics and even a fainting spell. Better have smelling salts handy and a nip of brandy.
clean: Be prepared for hysterics and even a fainting spell. Better have smelling salts handy and a nip of brandy.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Christine Vole: I do not think that will be necessary. I never faint because I am not sure that I will fall gracefully and I never use smelling salts because they puff up the eyes. I am Christine Vole.
clean: I do not think that will be necessary. I never faint because I am not sure that I will fall gracefully and I never use smelling salts because they puff up the eyes. I am Christine Vole.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: I'd better take that thermos of cocoa with me. It helps me wash down down the pills.
clean: I'd better take that thermos of cocoa with me. It helps me wash down down the pills.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Miss Plimsoll: Let me see. My learned patient is not above substituting brandy for cocoa. [opens thermos and smells] Sniff, sniff. It is cocoa. So sorry.
clean: Let me see. My learned patient is not above substituting brandy for cocoa. Sniff, sniff. It is cocoa. So sorry.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: If you were a woman, Miss Plimsoll, I would strike you.
clean: If you were a woman, Miss Plimsoll, I would strike you.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Janet Mackensie: Perhaps you can help me, your Lordship. Six months, I have applied for my hearing aid and I am still waiting for it.
clean: Perhaps you can help me, your Lordship. Six months, I have applied for my hearing aid and I am still waiting for it.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Judge: My dear Miss Mackenzie. Considering the rubbish that is being talked nowadays, you are missing very little.
clean: My dear Miss Mackenzie. Considering the rubbish that is being talked nowadays, you are missing very little.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: Mrs Vole. Or Mrs Helm; which do you prefer to be called?
clean: Mrs Vole. Or Mrs Helm; which do you prefer to be called?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Christine Helm: It does not matter.
clean: It does not matter.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: Does it not? In this country, we are inclined to take a rather more serious view of marriage. However, Frau Helm, it would appear that when you first met the prisoner in Hamburg, you lied to him about your marital status?
clean: Does it not? In this country, we are inclined to take a rather more serious view of marriage. However, Frau Helm, it would appear that when you first met the prisoner in Hamburg, you lied to him about your marital status?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Christine Helm: I wanted to get out of Germany, so—
clean: I wanted to get out of Germany, so-

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: You lied, did you not? Just yes or no, please.
clean: You lied, did you not? Just yes or no, please.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: Thank you. And subsequently, in arranging the marriage, you lied to the authorities?
clean: Thank you. And subsequently, in arranging the marriage, you lied to the authorities?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Christine Helm: I, um, did not tell the truth to the authorities.
clean: I, um, did not tell the truth to the authorities.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: You lied to them?
clean: You lied to them?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: And in the ceremony of marriage itself, when you swore to love and to honor and to cherish your husband, that, too, was a lie?
clean: And in the ceremony of marriage itself, when you swore to love and to honor and to cherish your husband, that, too, was a lie?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: And when the police questioned you about this wretched man who believed himself married and loved, you told them—
clean: And when the police questioned you about this wretched man who believed himself married and loved, you told them-

heading: Dialogue
raw: Christine Helm: I told them what Leonard wanted me to say.
clean: I told them what Leonard wanted me to say.

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: You told them that he was at home with you at 25 minutes past 9, and now you say that that was a lie? [beginning to chuckle now]
clean: You told them that he was at home with you at 25 minutes past 9, and now you say that that was a lie?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Christine Helm: Yes, a lie!
clean: Yes, a lie!

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: And when you said that he had accidentally cut his wrist, again, you lied? [chuckling again]
clean: And when you said that he had accidentally cut his wrist, again, you lied?

heading: Dialogue
raw: Sir Wilfrid: [chuckling further] And now today you've told us a new story entirely! [serious now] The question is, Frau Helm, were you lying then, are you lying now, or are you not in fact a chronic and habitual LIAR?!
clean: And now today you've told us a new story entirely! The question is, Frau Helm, were you lying then, are you lying now, or are you not in fact a chronic and habitual LIAR?!
